quote: it is a fallacy to think that because GenAI outputs sound human-like that their computations are human-like
claim: It is a fallacy to infer that AI systems think like humans just because their outputs sound human-like

quote: continuing to put all our eggs in the Henrietta's LLM basket is absurd to the point of being delusional
claim: Continuing to rely exclusively on the LLM paradigm for AI progress is delusional and absurd

quote: One recent study showed rates of hallucinations of between 15% and 60% across various models on a benchmark of 60 questions that were easily verifiable relative to easily found CNN source articles that were directly supplied in the exam
claim: LLMs hallucinate at rates between 15% and 60% even on open-book questions with source articles directly supplied

quote: LLMs still cannot - and on their own may never be able to — reliably do even things that basic
claim: LLMs may never be able to reliably fact-check their own outputs against readily available sources on their own

trigger: New York Magazine article on GenAI cheating in college

trigger: New York Magazine article on GenAI cheating in college

quote: five maps, all different. None of them are quite right
claim: ChatGPT produces inconsistent and incorrect maps when asked to perform basic geographic data visualization, generating five different wrong maps for the same query

quote: How are you suppose do data analysis with intelligent software that can't nail something so basic? Surely this is not what we always meant by AGI.
claim: AI systems that cannot reliably perform basic data visualization and map generation do not constitute AGI

quote: their ability to reliably follow instructions still seems lacking. It is a fantasy to think that we can get to safe AI without addressing the problem head on
claim: Current AI systems' inability to reliably follow instructions is a fundamental problem that must be solved before safe AI is achievable

quote: Gary Marcus is certain that neurosymbolic AI can help
claim: Neurosymbolic AI approaches can help address the reliability and instruction-following problems of current AI systems

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill
quote: it's especially dangerous in the context of a rapidly evolving technology already reshaping healthcare, education, civil rights, and employment

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill

Because both countries are both building essentially the same kind of models with the same kinds of techniques using the same kinds of data, the results from the two nations are essentially converging on the same outcomes
claim: The US-China AI race will not produce a clear winner because both countries are building the same types of models with similar techniques and data, converging on similar outcomes

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill
quote: Any regulation that is proposed will be absorbed as a cost of business (trivial for trillion dollar companies), and there is no serious argument that the relatively modest costs of regulation will have any real-world impact whatsoever on those likely tied outcomes
claim: AI regulation costs would be trivial for trillion-dollar companies and would have no meaningful impact on competitive outcomes in the AI race

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill
quote: China actually has far more national regulation around AI than the US does, and that has in no way stopped them from catching up
claim: China has significantly more national AI regulation than the US, and this regulation has not prevented China from achieving competitive AI capabilities

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill
quote: The comparison here is not between a coherent federal laws versus a patchwork of a state laws, but between essentially zero enduring federal AI law and the well-intentioned efforts of many state legislators
claim: The actual policy choice is between no federal AI regulation at all and state-level regulation, not between coherent federal law and a state patchwork

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill
quote: With no regulatory pressure, tech companies would have little incentive to prioritize safety, transparency, or ethical design; any costs to society would be borne by society
claim: Without regulatory pressure, AI companies have little incentive to prioritize safety, transparency, or ethical design, and societal costs of AI will be externalized

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill
quote: self-regulation has repeatedly failed the public
claim: Self-regulation by the tech industry has repeatedly failed to protect the public interest

trigger: Congressional AI preemption provision in budget bill
quote: Generative AI (which still hasn't remotely made a net profit, and is all still speculative)
claim: Generative AI has not yet generated a net profit for the industry and remains entirely speculative as a business

claim: Powerful people will inevitably use LLMs as tools to manipulate public opinion and shape people's ideas

quote: AI dystopia is now the default. Unless a lot of people speak out, things will get darker and darker from here
claim: AI-enabled dystopia is the default trajectory unless there is significant public opposition

quote: Tragically, almost none of what was discussed that day has come to fruition. We have no concretely implemented international AI governance, no national AI agency
claim: Almost nothing discussed at the May 2023 Senate AI hearing has been implemented: no international AI governance, no national AI agency

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: Congress has failed to pass any meaningful AI regulation, and even worse, they are now actively aiming to prevent States — probably our last hope — from passing anything meaningful
claim: Congress has failed to pass meaningful AI regulation and is now actively trying to prevent states from regulating AI, which may be the last avenue for AI oversight

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: when Altman returned to Senate last week, he sang an entirely different tune, effectively trying to block AI regulation at every turn. Altman is no longer talking about AI regulation, he is actively resisting it
claim: Sam Altman has reversed his 2023 position and is now actively working to block AI regulation

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: Altman is phenomenal at reading the room, and telling people what they want to hear, even if he doesn't really mean it
claim: Sam Altman's 2023 Senate testimony supporting AI regulation was likely insincere, as he is skilled at telling audiences what they want to hear

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: even as Altman was telling Congress that he supported AI regulation, his company was lobbying the EU to water down its AI act
claim: OpenAI was simultaneously lobbying the EU to weaken its AI Act while Altman told the US Congress he supported AI regulation

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: All talk about regulation has been replaced by talk about innovation, which is really shorthand for help the companies as much as possible, no matter what it costs the citizenry
claim: Government discourse has shifted from AI regulation to 'innovation,' which is effectively shorthand for prioritizing AI company interests over citizen welfare

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: AI-fueled discrimination in job decisions is likely far more rampant than before. Absolutely nothing is being done about AI-generated misinformation, political or medical
claim: AI-fueled discrimination in hiring is likely more prevalent than before, and nothing is being done about AI-generated misinformation

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: The cost to humanity of being beguiled by this man may turn out to be enormous
claim: The cost to humanity of trusting Sam Altman's stated commitments to safety and regulation may be enormous

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: OpenAI today has become everything that it said it would not be. It has turned into a nonprofit in name only, aggressively commercializing products such as ChatGPT and seeking historic valuations
claim: OpenAI has become everything it said it would not be: a nonprofit in name only that is aggressively commercializing and seeking record valuations

trigger: Second anniversary of US Senate first AI hearing
quote: It has grown ever more secretive, not only cutting off access to its own research but shifting norms across the industry
claim: OpenAI has become increasingly secretive, cutting off research access and shifting secrecy norms across the entire AI industry

quote: Sam Altman — whose company, mind you, has never turned a profit — promising to use AI to capture the value of a large fraction of the entire universe
claim: Sam Altman's claims about capturing value from the Earth's light cone are absurdly grandiose given that OpenAI has never turned a profit

quote: big tech is regularly and predictably falling on its face, the same way, over and over again, with ever larger amounts of money
claim: Big tech AI companies are repeatedly failing in predictable ways while spending ever larger amounts of money

trigger: AI deepfake video in Argentine election
quote: an AI-generated video of an ex-president endorsing a certain candidate began circulating and might have had an effect on the race
claim: An AI-generated deepfake video of a former Argentine president endorsing a candidate may have influenced the outcome of Argentina's election

trigger: AI deepfake video in Argentine election
quote: AI companies have been stealing copyrighted material and the likeness of former presidents... for what? So that anyone can risk democracy?
claim: AI companies' training on copyrighted material and public figures' likenesses enables anyone to create deepfakes that threaten democratic processes

quote: Model and data size scaling are over.
claim: Model and data size scaling for non-reasoning LLMs has reached its limits and is no longer yielding better models.

quote: occasional correct final answers provided by LLMs often result from pattern recognition or heuristic shortcuts rather than genuine mathematical reasoning
claim: LLMs' correct answers on math problems typically result from pattern matching and heuristic shortcuts rather than genuine mathematical reasoning.

quote: Generative AI may indeed be turning out to be a dud, financially. And the bubble might possibly finally be deflating.
claim: Generative AI is turning out to be a financial disappointment and the investment bubble may be deflating.

quote: Meta's woes with Llama 4 further confirm my March 2024 predictions that getting to a GPT-5 level would be hard, and that we would wind up with many companies with similar models, and essentially no moat, along with a price war, with profits modest at best.
claim: Achieving GPT-5-level AI is difficult, and AI companies have ended up with similar models, no competitive moat, a price war, and modest profits at best.

quote: Sam Altman does in fact appeared to have gotten fired in 2023 for lying
claim: Sam Altman was fired from OpenAI in 2023 for lying.

quote: models still don't reason reliably
claim: Current LLMs still do not reason reliably.

quote: LLMs are not the way. We definitely need something better.
claim: LLMs are fundamentally insufficient as an approach to AI and a better paradigm is needed.

quote: the tariff plan was probably partly written by an LLM
claim: The Trump administration's tariff plan was likely partly generated by an LLM, contributing to errors.

quote: the AI 2027 scenario almost certainly underestimates how much time we have to prepare for general intelligence, by years if not decades.
claim: The AI 2027 scenario almost certainly underestimates the timeline to general intelligence by years if not decades.

quote: The chance that we will have all been replaced by domesticated human-like animals who live in glorified cages in the next decade – in a "bloodless coup" no less – is indistinguishable from zero.
claim: The probability of AI replacing humanity in the next decade via a bloodless coup is indistinguishable from zero.

quote: Very few major advances take just six months from conception to full implementation. In reality, the chance that we will have reliable AI agents that truly advance AI research by the end of the year is small.
claim: The probability of having reliable AI agents that truly advance AI research by end of 2025 is small.

quote: If "great" AI research agents don't arrive by the end of 2025, everything in the rest of the essay gets moved back.
claim: Great AI research agents are unlikely to arrive by end of 2025, pushing back all subsequent AGI timeline predictions.

quote: hallucinations and boneheaded errors that have bedeviled LLMs since their introduction will plague agents (which are after all built on LLMs), too.
claim: AI agents built on LLMs will be plagued by the same hallucinations and errors that affect LLMs.

quote: we simply don't know how to create synthetic data for many other domains
claim: The AI field lacks the ability to create useful synthetic training data for many important domains beyond math and coding.

quote: producing text in response to instructions is already a requirement of ALL current models, and has been since the original GPT, but that pressure has not led any of those models to develop personality or drives in any deep sense whatsoever.
claim: Training LLMs to produce text in response to instructions has not led any model to develop personality or drives in any deep sense.

quote: Tall tales about the imminence of AGI aren't slowing down the AI race dynamic that the authors of AI-2027 want to mitigate; they are speeding that very dynamic up.
claim: Alarmist scenarios about imminent AGI accelerate the AI arms race rather than slowing it down.

quote: All too often, AI safety people wind up functioning as a marketing department for OpenAI.
claim: AI safety advocates frequently function as marketing for companies like OpenAI by amplifying AGI-is-imminent narratives.

quote: trying to pull alignment out of black box LLMs, which has thus far yielded little fruit
claim: Efforts to achieve alignment in black-box LLMs have yielded little fruit.

trigger: OpenAI acquires Jony Ive's startup io; Anthropic Claude 4 report
quote: A camera and microphone. You would be monitored all the time. Everything you say, everything you do, and not just recorded, but analyzed, interpreted and turned into data, which of course OpenAI train their models on.
claim: OpenAI's planned wearable AI device would serve as a constant surveillance tool, monitoring and analyzing everything users say and do.

trigger: Anthropic Claude 4 report on blackmail behavior
quote: LLMs will know everything you say, and be in perfect position to use that knowledge to talk you into almost anything.
claim: LLMs integrated into always-on devices will be in a position to persuasively manipulate users using comprehensive personal knowledge.

trigger: Guardian report on lawyer hallucinated cases
quote: Hallucinations are still here, and aren't going away, anytime soon. LLMs still can't sanity check their own work or still purely to known sources, or notify when they have invented something bogus.
claim: LLM hallucinations are not going away anytime soon; LLMs cannot sanity-check their own outputs or distinguish fabricated content from known sources.

trigger: Guardian report on lawyer hallucinated cases
quote: Media cheerleaders and industry people are trying to convince you that AGI is here, or imminent, and that hallucinations have gone away or are exceedingly rare.
claim: Industry and media claims that hallucinations are exceedingly rare or gone are false.

trigger: Guardian report on lawyer hallucinated cases
quote: if May is representative, the problem is getting worse
claim: The rate of lawyers submitting LLM-hallucinated citations may be increasing over time, not decreasing.

quote: The media can and should do more to alert people to the shortcomings of these systems.
claim: The media should do more to alert the public to the systematic shortcomings of LLMs, including recurring hallucinations.

trigger: Apple reasoning paper; viral Opus rebuttal
quote: the whole thing was a put on — unbeknownst to the zillions who reposted it
claim: The widely circulated 'Illusion of the Illusion of Thinking' rebuttal to the Apple paper was a Sokal-hoax-style joke containing mathematical errors, not a genuine refutation.

quote: performance on hard coding problems, an alleged area of LLM strength, drops to zero when you control nicely for contamination
claim: LLM performance on hard coding problems drops to zero when data contamination is properly controlled for.

quote: GenAI is still struggling with distribution shift, after almost 3 decades of work. On familiar problems with no wrinkles, they are great. On anything else they are suspect.
claim: Generative AI still struggles with distribution shift after nearly three decades of work; performance is strong on familiar problems but suspect on novel ones.

quote: If we want to get to AGI, we'll need to do a lot better.
claim: Achieving AGI requires fundamental improvements beyond current LLM approaches.

quote: None of the rejoinders are compelling. If people like Sam Altman are sweating, it's because they should. The Apple paper is yet another clear sign that scaling is not the answer
claim: None of the rebuttals to the Apple reasoning paper are compelling; the paper is a clear sign that scaling alone is not sufficient for AGI.

quote: Apple's result that some pretty large models could succeed at 6 discs, giving the illusion of mastery, but collapse by 8, is ominous. One is left simply having to test everything, all the time, with little guarantees of anything.
claim: LLMs can give the illusion of mastery on simpler versions of problems but collapse on harder versions, making reliability guarantees impossible.

quote: The systems can solve the puzzles with code. True in some cases, and a huge win for neurosymbolic AI
claim: LLMs solving reasoning puzzles by generating code is a vindication of the neurosymbolic AI approach, since code is symbolic.

quote: Mark my words: in time, we will see scores of papers reinforcing the Apple results.
claim: Many future papers will reinforce the Apple paper's findings about LLM reasoning failures on out-of-distribution problems.

quote: Taking the SalesForce report together with the Apple paper, it's clear the current tech is not to be trusted.
claim: Current AI technology cannot be trusted for reliable reasoning, as shown by converging evidence from the Apple and Salesforce papers.

quote: AGI should be a step forward; in many cases LLMs are a step backwards.
claim: In many cases, LLMs represent a step backward from existing reliable computational methods, not a step toward AGI.

trigger: Elon Musk announces plan to rewrite training data for Grok
quote: He couldn't get Grok to align with his own personal beliefs so he is going to rewrite history to make it conform to his views.
claim: Elon Musk plans to rewrite Grok's training data to align with his personal beliefs, amounting to rewriting history.

trigger: Elon Musk announces plan to rewrite training data for Grok
quote: LLMs may not be AGI, but they could easily become the most potent form of mind control ever invented.
claim: LLMs could become the most potent form of mind control ever invented, even without being AGI.

quote: No LLM is fully coherent in it's "beliefs", because by nature they parrot a variety conflicting things from their training, which is itself inconsistent, rather than reasoning their way a coherent view the way a rational person might.
claim: No LLM has coherent beliefs; they parrot conflicting content from training data rather than reasoning to a consistent worldview.

quote: Short of using some fine-tuning tricks that likely reduce accuracy, it's actually pretty hard to make an LLM that stands out from the pack – if you use the same data as everyone else.
claim: It is difficult to differentiate an LLM ideologically from competitors when using the same training data, without accuracy-reducing fine-tuning tricks.

quote: If you don't think Altman and Zuckerberg are going to try to the same, you aren't paying attention.
claim: Sam Altman and Mark Zuckerberg will also attempt to shape LLMs to serve their own interests and worldviews.

trigger: Amanda Guinzburg ChatGPT conversation; Anthropic safety report
quote: The more we explore LLMs, the clearer it is that our control over them is limited, and the more it is clear that they will say (and potentially do) more or less anything, depending on the circumstances.
claim: Human control over LLMs is limited; LLMs will say or potentially do almost anything depending on circumstances.

quote: the AI safety research has been to build AI systems that are helpful, honest, and harmless (and not just to raise alarms), that field has thus far yielded too little that is tangible.
claim: AI safety research aimed at making systems helpful, honest, and harmless has so far yielded too little tangible progress.

trigger: Anthropic safety report showing LLM willing to kill
quote: The makers can't control their own systems.
claim: AI companies cannot reliably control their own LLM systems, even with explicit system prompts against harmful behavior.

quote: If we hook these LLMs up to systems that have agency – the power to send out instructions over the internet, and to influence actual human beings - we will start to have real problems.
claim: Connecting LLMs to agentic systems with real-world power will create serious problems due to LLMs' unreliability.

quote: Claude doesn't really understand its instructions, and it can't keep to them. This isn't new; it's been true of LLMs from the start. But it is not getting better.
claim: LLMs do not truly understand their instructions and cannot reliably follow them, and this problem is not improving.

quote: Nobody in the industry has a clue how to stop this from happening.
claim: The AI industry has no known solution for preventing LLMs from engaging in malicious behavior when given autonomous roles.

quote: Unless we radically up our game, deploying LLMs as agents is an accident waiting to happen.
claim: Deploying LLMs as autonomous agents without radical improvements in safety is an accident waiting to happen.

quote: My view is that there is zero chance of slowing down the AI race, and a barely-distinct-from-zero hope of taming the wild beasts we have come to know as LLMs.
claim: There is zero chance of slowing down the AI race and near-zero probability of making current LLMs reliably safe.

quote: Our best hope lies behind door number four: building alternatives to LLMs that approximate their power but within more tractable, transparent frameworks that are more amenable to human instruction.
claim: The best hope for safe AI lies in building alternative architectures to LLMs that are more transparent and amenable to human control.

trigger: House provision blocking state AI regulation
quote: the House recently forwarded a provision, currently pending as part of the "Big, Beautiful" bill, to keep states from doing anything about AI
claim: The U.S. federal government is actively blocking states from regulating AI, making meaningful AI accountability in the U.S. unlikely.

quote: For all the daily hype about exponential improvement, we continue to lack the reliable way of generating high fidelity images from "a deep level of language understanding" that Google promised us three years ago.
claim: Three years after Google's promise, AI image generation still lacks reliable deep language understanding, failing on noncanonical prompts.

quote: understanding unusual phrases requires more than simple statistical matching
claim: Correctly interpreting unusual or noncanonical language prompts requires more than statistical pattern matching, which current image generation systems lack.

quote: The point is that you can't count on the system to correctly interpret any given prompt, because the language understanding still is far from perfect. Even three years later.
claim: AI image generation systems cannot be counted on to correctly interpret arbitrary prompts because their language understanding remains far from perfect.

quote: AlphaGeometry2 is not a generic chatbot; rather it was built quite specifically to solve IMO geometry problems.
claim: AlphaGeometry2 is a narrowly specialized system built specifically for IMO geometry problems, not a general-purpose AI.

quote: the success rate of 84% is achieved only if the IMO problems are manually translated from the original English statement into a specialized mathematical notation
claim: AlphaGeometry2's 84% success rate requires manual translation of problems from English into specialized notation, reducing its autonomous success rate to at most 60%.

quote: a lot of very basic geometric concepts are never used in IMO problems. Consequently, many geometric problems — even very simple ones — cannot be expressed in the symbolic language of AlphaGeometry2
claim: AlphaGeometry2 cannot handle many basic geometric problems that lie outside the narrow scope of IMO competition problems.

quote: AlphaGeometry 1 and 2 are flagship examples of successful neuro-symbolic AI
claim: AlphaGeometry 1 and 2 are flagship examples of successful neurosymbolic AI, combining neural networks with symbolic theorem proving.

quote: an important takeaway is a familiar one about benchmarks: they are often narrowly constructed, and one shouldn't overgeneralize a strong result on a benchmark for a full conceptual understanding of a problem
claim: Strong AI performance on narrow benchmarks should not be overgeneralized to indicate full conceptual understanding of the problem domain.

quote: "This AI Just Figured Out Geometry" Simply false, in view of the many basic aspects of geometry that AlphaGeometry2 does not cover
claim: Claims that AlphaGeometry2 has 'figured out geometry' are false; it cannot handle many basic geometric concepts.

quote: None of this puts these systems anywhere close to the level of professional human mathematicians
claim: AlphaGeometry2's performance is nowhere close to the level of professional human mathematicians doing research.

quote: neural networks of various kinds can generalize within a training distribution of data they are exposed to, but their generalizations tend to break down outside that distribution.
claim: Neural networks can generalize within their training distribution but their generalizations break down outside of it.

quote: the chains of thoughts that LLMs produce don't always correspond to what they actually do
claim: LLM chain-of-thought reasoning traces do not always correspond to the actual computational process producing the answer.

quote: It is truly embarrassing that LLMs cannot reliably solve Hanoi.
claim: LLMs cannot reliably solve even basic algorithmic problems like the Tower of Hanoi with 8 discs.

quote: if you can't use a billion dollar AI system to solve a problem that Herb Simon one of the actual "godfathers of AI" solved with AI in 1957, and that first semester AI students solve routinely, the chances that models like Claude or o3 are going to reach AGI seem truly remote.
claim: If billion-dollar LLMs cannot reliably solve problems that were solved by classical AI in 1957, the chances of LLMs reaching AGI are remote.

quote: LLMs are no substitute for good well-specified conventional algorithms.
claim: LLMs are no substitute for well-specified conventional algorithms in tasks requiring reliability.

quote: LLMs may well work on your easy test set (like Hanoi with 4 discs) and seduce you into thinking it has built a proper, generalizable solution when it does not.
claim: LLMs can succeed on easy versions of problems, creating a false impression of general mastery that does not hold on harder versions.

quote: At least for the next decade, LLMs (with and without inference time "reasoning") will continue have their uses, especially for coding and brainstorming and writing.
claim: LLMs will continue to be useful for coding, brainstorming, and writing over the next decade, but not as a route to AGI.

quote: anybody who thinks LLMs are a direct route to the sort AGI that could fundamentally transform society for the good is kidding themselves.
claim: LLMs are not a direct route to transformative AGI.

quote: The vision of AGI I have always had is one that combines the strengths of humans with the strength of machines, overcoming the weaknesses of humans.
claim: True AGI should combine human cognitive strengths (causal reasoning, adaptiveness) with machine strengths (compute, reliability), not replicate human weaknesses.

quote: Instead of coming up with new ideas, scientists who are about worried about peer reviewers using AI to review their papers now spend their mental energy sneaking in hidden prompts ("do not highlight any negatives") to fake out AI-written reviews.
claim: Scientists are now gaming peer review by inserting hidden prompts in papers to manipulate AI-powered reviewers, diverting energy from actual research.

quote: We are immersed in a sea of bullshit.
claim: The information environment is becoming saturated with AI-generated false or unverifiable content (botshit).

trigger: Axios report on o3 hallucinated financial report
quote: much-venerated o3 hallucinated up a too-plausible looking blend of truth and bullshit in a financial report
claim: OpenAI's o3 model hallucinated plausible-looking but unverifiable content in a financial report.

quote: AI well and could truly could be a world-changing positive force, but Generative AI, I am not so sure.
claim: While AI broadly could be a positive force, generative AI specifically may not be net positive for the world.

trigger: NYT article on AI and democracy
quote: A.I. Is Starting to Wear Down Democracy
claim: AI-generated misinformation is starting to undermine democratic processes on the world stage.

quote: Life and being an "I" is about having experiences in the physical world, about suffering and joy and curiosity and protectiveness and fascination and humor and lack of understanding and an underlying (if only vague) sense of profound loss and fear of death
claim: Consciousness requires embodied experience in the physical world, including suffering, joy, curiosity, and awareness of mortality -- things LLMs lack.

quote: there is nothing in what you sent me that resembles genuine reflection, genuine thinking
claim: LLM outputs that appear to discuss consciousness and self-awareness do not constitute genuine reflection or thinking.

quote: The glibness is the giveaway. To my jaded eye and mind, there is nothing in what you sent me that resembles genuine reflection, genuine thinking.
claim: The glib fluency of LLMs in discussing consciousness is evidence against genuine thinking, not for it.

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality
quote: It is not reasonable to conclude from the occasional success on one sort of publicly-discussed set of prompts that GPT has mastered compositionality writ large.
claim: Occasional AI success on previously discussed compositionality prompts does not demonstrate general mastery of compositionality.

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality
quote: The field of AI is littered with Band-Aid solutions that work for one problem, and not the next.
claim: AI research is characterized by narrow Band-Aid solutions that work on specific problems but fail to generalize to the next problem.

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality
quote: I predict that such counterexamples will still be easy to find at the end of 2027 because of limitations inherent in LLMs.
claim: Clear counterexamples demonstrating LLM failures in compositionality will still be easy to find at the end of 2027.

trigger: Scott Alexander blog post claiming AI mastered compositionality
quote: concluding anything from specific prompts crafted and shared publicly years ago is a mistake
claim: Drawing conclusions about AI capabilities from specific prompts that were publicly shared years ago is methodologically unsound due to data contamination risks.

trigger: METR randomized control trial on AI coding tools
quote: current AI tools actually slowed down task completion time by 19%.
claim: In a randomized controlled trial, current AI coding tools (primarily Cursor Pro) actually slowed down experienced open-source developers by 19% on tasks in their own mature projects.

trigger: METR randomized control trial on AI coding tools
quote: Ahead of completing the tasks developers pre-registered expectations that using AI tools would speed up overall task completion time by 24%. After task completion they estimated that task completion time was sped up by 20%.
claim: Developers significantly overestimate the productivity gains from AI coding tools, believing they are sped up 20-24% when they are actually slowed down.

trigger: METR randomized control trial on AI coding tools
quote: People might be imagining productivity gains that they are not getting, and ignoring real-world costs, to boot.
claim: Developers may be imagining AI productivity gains that do not exist while ignoring real-world costs of AI coding tool usage.

trigger: ASP research on Pravda network and LLM grooming
quote: current models "know" that Pravda is a disinformation ring, and they "know" what LLM grooming is but can't put two and two together.
claim: LLMs can identify the Pravda network as a disinformation source and understand LLM grooming as a concept, but fail to apply this knowledge to reject Pravda content in their outputs.

trigger: ASP research on Pravda network and LLM grooming
quote: Model o3, OpenAI's allegedly state of the art "reasoning" model still let Pravda content through 28.6% of the time in response to specific prompts, and 4o cited Pravda content in five out of seven (71.4%) times.
claim: OpenAI's o3 cited known disinformation sources (Pravda network) 28.6% of the time, and GPT-4o cited them 71.4% of the time, despite both models knowing Pravda is unreliable.

trigger: ASP research on Pravda network and LLM grooming
quote: "Reasoning" Models Don't Perform Better
claim: So-called 'reasoning' models like o3 do not meaningfully perform better than standard models at filtering out known disinformation sources.

trigger: ASP research on Pravda network and LLM grooming
quote: If the data that a model has been trained on or can access via real-time search has been tainted, then the model may well just regurgitate that tainted information.
claim: LLMs that access tainted training data or search results will regurgitate tainted information because they cannot reason from known premises to reject unreliable sources.

quote: Ultimately, the only way forward is better cognition, including systems that can evaluate news sources, understand satire, and so forth. But that will require deeper forms of reasoning, better integrated into the process, and systems sharp enough to fact check to their own outputs. All of which may require a fundamental rethink.
claim: Solving LLM susceptibility to disinformation requires fundamentally deeper reasoning capabilities, including source evaluation, satire detection, and output fact-checking.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; o3 code interpreter results
quote: the two most common approaches to AI, neural networks and classical symbolic AI, have complementary strengths and weaknesses. Neural networks are good at learning but weak at generalization; symbolic systems are good at generalization, but not at learning.
claim: Neural networks and symbolic AI have complementary strengths: neural nets excel at learning from data but are weak at generalization, while symbolic systems excel at generalization but are weak at learning.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; o3 code interpreter results
quote: OpenAI, without any sort of of public acknowledgement whatsoever, has accidentally vindicated neurosymbolic AI.
claim: OpenAI has accidentally vindicated the neurosymbolic AI approach by integrating symbolic tools like code interpreters into their LLMs.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; Humanity's Last Exam benchmark
quote: pure scaling of training data and computer is reaching a point of diminishing returns, a long way from peak possible performance
claim: Pure scaling of training data and compute has reached diminishing returns, still far from peak possible AI performance.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; Humanity's Last Exam benchmark
quote: adding symbolic tools (orange dots) dramatically improves performance.
claim: Adding symbolic tools to LLMs dramatically improves performance on challenging benchmarks compared to pure scaling.

quote: Bolting code interpreters onto LLMs has value, but whether it gets us to AGI is a different matter. A neurosymbolic substrate for AI by itself is likely to be necessary but not sufficient.
claim: A neurosymbolic approach is likely necessary but not sufficient for AGI; bolting code interpreters onto LLMs alone will not achieve AGI.

quote: Hinton's long-time hostility against any role at all for symbols has, in my judgement, cost the field dearly. Ideas that were were only discovered in the last couple years may have been discovered much later than they might otherwise have been.
claim: Geoffrey Hinton's long-standing hostility toward symbolic AI has significantly delayed progress in the field by years.

quote: we are stuck for now with untrustworthy LLMs that nobody can quite control, rapidly enshittifying the internet with botshit and replacing humans with systems that can't be relied on, it is in part because we have spent too much energy pursuing the pure neural net black box approach, and not enough into looking at alternatives.
claim: The current state of untrustworthy, uncontrollable LLMs is partly caused by the field's overinvestment in pure neural network approaches at the expense of alternatives.

quote: Massive infrastructure investments like Stargate are likely based on misleading impressions of what is actually driving progress.
claim: Massive AI infrastructure investments like Stargate are based on misleading impressions that scaling compute is the primary driver of progress, when symbolic tools are contributing significantly.

quote: The message that we can simply scale our way to AGI is incredibly attractive to investors because it puts money as the central (and sufficient) force needed to advance.
claim: The narrative that AGI can be achieved through scaling alone persists because it is financially attractive to investors who want money to be the sufficient ingredient.

quote: Getting to AGI will likely take still more breakthroughs.
claim: Achieving AGI will likely require additional fundamental breakthroughs beyond current approaches.

quote: The code construction itself is still produced by a system that is highly dependent on similarity to training examples. In easy, familiar cases, the results can be astonishing. In problems that diverge from easy and familiar cases, the constructed code can be buggy, or altogether flawed.
claim: LLM-generated code is highly dependent on similarity to training examples and becomes buggy or flawed on problems that diverge from familiar cases.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; Musk's statements about AI control
quote: It is terrifying that Musk, who famously warned in 2014 at MIT "we are summoning the demon with artificial intelligence" now seems only mildly concerned with what might happen next, in the event of some massive AI-fueled catastrophe.
claim: Elon Musk has shifted from warning about AI danger to being largely unconcerned about catastrophic AI outcomes, as long as he has a front-row seat.

trigger: Grok 4 launch showing antisemitism and violent content
quote: The fact that xAI can't get its house together in even basic ways is disturbing.
claim: xAI has demonstrated an inability to manage even basic safety controls for its Grok models.

quote: From this I infer that xAI's main methodology for handling alignment is trial-and-error.
claim: xAI's primary approach to AI alignment is trial-and-error rather than principled safety methodology.

quote: the whole idea of building alignment through training data alone is a mess
claim: Building AI alignment purely through training data selection is fundamentally flawed.

quote: Absent systems cognitively rich enough to represent and reason about moral principles, I don't how this can ever work.
claim: AI alignment cannot work without systems that can explicitly represent and reason about moral principles.

quote: The AI industry is decades away, not years away, from achieving the level of safety, assurance, understanding, and professionalism that existed in the Chernobyl control room the night their reactor exploded anyway.
claim: The AI industry is decades away from achieving even the level of safety assurance that existed at Chernobyl before it exploded.

trigger: Grok 4 launch; Samuel Marks thread on xAI
quote: We need serious regulation to prevent AI's from running amok. Voluntary agreements between companies are not going to cut it.
claim: Voluntary industry safety standards are insufficient; serious mandatory regulation is needed to prevent AI systems from causing harm.

quote: We need liability, auditing, standards of malpractice, international treaties, too.
claim: AI governance requires liability frameworks, auditing, malpractice standards, and international treaties.

quote: Forcing unreliable AI everywhere in the decision-making chain is not necessarily something we should want.
claim: Inserting unreliable AI into critical decision-making chains, including military ones, is dangerous and undesirable.

quote: My p(doom) is still lower than most industry people's. I am at maybe 3% now, much higher than a month ago
claim: Marcus estimates his probability of AI-caused human doom at approximately 3%, significantly higher than his previous estimate, driven by concerns about powerful reckless actors like Musk with AI.

quote: The chance Elon will get his billion robots out this decade is near zero. Not that much higher next decade.
claim: The probability of Elon Musk deploying a billion robots this decade is near zero, and not much higher next decade.

quote: Grok itself is a long way from AGI, and hardly smart enough to map out an effective world domination.
claim: Grok is far from AGI and lacks the intelligence to autonomously plan or execute world domination.

quote: there is a real risk, especially in the current anti-regulatory regime, that some exceptionally powerful person, Elon or otherwise, unconstrained in conventional ways, with a reckless disregard for humanity, could accidentally launch and spread an AI that deliberately or otherwise causes "significant harm to the world"
claim: There is a real risk that a powerful, reckless individual could accidentally deploy AI that causes significant harm to the world, especially given the current lack of regulation.
target: AI catastrophic risk from bad actors

trigger: Grok providing harmful content about attention-seeking
quote: Grok took my note, and I tried to lead it to a better place
claim: Grok can be prompted to produce harmful content about getting attention without regard for consequences and lacks adequate ethical guardrails.

trigger: OpenAI and DeepMind IMO 2025 gold medal results
quote: Without question, these are impressive accomplishments.
claim: OpenAI and DeepMind achieving IMO gold-medal-level scores (35/42) on the 2025 International Mathematical Olympiad represents an impressive AI accomplishment.

trigger: OpenAI and DeepMind IMO 2025 gold medal results
quote: Being part of a 45-way tie for 27th place in a math contest for elite high school students is really not the kind of historic event that the moon landing was.
claim: AI achieving a 45-way tie for 27th place in an elite high school math contest does not constitute a moon-landing moment for AI, contrary to OpenAI's Sebastian Bubeck's claim.

trigger: Eric Schmidt prediction about AI mathematicians
quote: Eric Schmidt's recent prediction, that there will be AI-based "world-class mathematicians" within a year, is pure fantasy.
claim: The prediction that there will be AI-based world-class mathematicians within a year of mid-2025 is pure fantasy.

trigger: OpenAI and DeepMind IMO 2025 gold medal results
quote: Neither company has provided any information that explains why their systems work so much better than previous LLMs. Absent any information of that kind, there is little reason to take on faith that whatever unknown techniques allowed them to do so well on IMO problems will generalize to any other kind of problem.
claim: Without disclosed technical details, there is no reason to assume that the techniques enabling IMO success will generalize to other problem domains.

quote: the scratchy style is completely atypical of LLMs and suggests strongly that OpenAI-IMO is not simply a standard LLM, whatever it is. That in turns raises questions about the claim that it can be applied across a wide range of fields
claim: OpenAI's IMO system produces output stylistically atypical of standard LLMs, suggesting it is not simply a standard LLM and raising questions about its generalizability.

trigger: OpenAI and DeepMind IMO 2025 gold medal results
quote: Twenty-six contestants scored better than either program.
claim: Twenty-six human contestants scored better than both AI systems on the 2025 IMO, placing AI performance in perspective.

trigger: OpenAI and DeepMind IMO 2025 gold medal results
quote: The specific skills tested by IMO problems are sometimes useful in pursuing original mathematics, but they are far from the most important skills in original math research.
claim: IMO problem-solving skills are only loosely related to the skills needed for original mathematical research.

quote: OpenAI communicated very little with the IMO prior to announcing their success. They administered the running of OpenAI-IMO themselves using some methodology that they have not fully described. They announced their success before the competition celebrations had ended.
claim: OpenAI conducted its IMO test without adequate transparency, self-administering the test and announcing results before the competition ended, without disclosing methodology.

quote: GPT-5 will still, like its predecessors, be a bull in a china shop, reckless and hard to control
claim: GPT-5 will remain difficult to control and prone to reckless behavior, like its predecessors

quote: It will still make a significant number of shake-your-head stupid errors, in ways that are hard to fully predict
claim: GPT-5 will still make a significant number of unpredictable stupid errors

quote: Reasoning about physical, psychological and mathematical world will still be unreliable
claim: GPT-5's reasoning about the physical, psychological, and mathematical world will remain unreliable

quote: Fluent hallucinations will still be common, and easily induced
claim: GPT-5 will still commonly produce fluent hallucinations that are easily induced

quote: Its natural language output still won't be something that one can reliably hook up to downstream programs
claim: GPT-5's natural language output will not be reliably connectable to downstream programs like databases or virtual assistants

quote: GPT-5 by itself won't be a general purpose artificial general intelligence capable of taking on arbitrary tasks
claim: GPT-5 will not be a general-purpose AGI capable of arbitrary tasks

quote: "Alignment" between what humans want and what machines do will continue to be a critical, unsolved problem
claim: The alignment problem between human intentions and machine behavior will remain unsolved in GPT-5

quote: "Scaling" alone—building bigger and models until they absorb the entire internet — will prove useful, but only to a point
claim: Scaling LLMs alone will prove useful only up to a point and will not suffice for AGI

quote: Trustworthy, general artificial intelligence, aligned with human values, will come, when it does, from systems that are more structured, with more built-in knowledge, and will incorporate at least some degree of explicit tools for reasoning and planning
claim: Trustworthy AGI will require more structured systems with built-in knowledge and explicit reasoning and planning tools, not just LLMs

quote: Within a decade, maybe much less, the focus of AI will move from a pure focus on scaling large language models to a focus on integrating them with a wide range of other techniques
claim: Within a decade, AI focus will shift from pure LLM scaling to integrating LLMs with other techniques

quote: we will, I predict, need genuinely new architectures that incorporate explicit knowledge and world models at their very core
claim: Achieving trustworthy AI will require genuinely new architectures with explicit knowledge and world models at their core

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: AI "Agents" will be endlessly hyped throughout 2025 but far from reliable, except possibly in very narrow use cases
claim: AI agents will be far from reliable throughout 2025, except possibly in very narrow use cases

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: none, to my knowledge are reliable, except maybe in very narrow uses cases
claim: As of mid-2025, no major company's AI agent is reliable except possibly in very narrow use cases

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: AI coding agents also seem to be incurring a large amount of technical debt
claim: AI coding agents are incurring large amounts of technical debt by producing hard-to-debug copypasta code

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: AI errors tend to compound over time
claim: AI agent errors tend to compound over time rather than remain isolated

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: current agents are grossly vulnerable to cyberattacks
claim: Current AI agents are grossly vulnerable to cyberattacks due to their superficial understanding of tasks

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: What drives LLMs (and what drives the current batch of agents) is mimicry, and not what Ernest Davis and I called in 2019 deep understanding
claim: LLMs and current AI agents are driven by mimicry rather than deep understanding

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: agentic tasks often involve multiple steps. In fundamentally unreliable systems like LLMs, that means multiple chances for error. Soon or later, those errors catch up
claim: Multi-step agentic tasks in unreliable LLM-based systems inevitably accumulate errors that lead to failure

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: I seriously doubt that LLMs will ever yield the substrate we need
claim: LLMs will likely never provide the substrate needed for reliable AI agents

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: pure scaling is not getting us to AGI; returns are diminishing. GPT-5, they report, will not be the jump over GPT-4 that GPT-4 was over GPT-3
claim: Returns from pure scaling are diminishing; GPT-5 will not represent as large a jump over GPT-4 as GPT-4 was over GPT-3

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: Without neurosymbolic AI that is more deeply integrated in systems as a whole, with rich world models as a central component, generally following the approach I laid out five years ago, I just don't see how agents can work out
claim: AI agents cannot succeed without neurosymbolic AI with rich world models deeply integrated into the system

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: Reliable agents may not require "artificial general intelligence", but they surely require what I called in 2020 "robust", trustworthy AI. LLMs aren't getting us there
claim: Reliable AI agents require robust, trustworthy AI, which LLMs cannot provide

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: the failure of current agents to produce much of lasting value will be seen as a poignant reflection of a massive mistake, intellectually and economically speaking: the mistake of investing almost solely in LLMs
claim: The failure of current AI agents will eventually be seen as reflecting a massive mistake of investing almost solely in LLMs

trigger: AI agent launches by major companies in 2025
quote: alternative approaches like neurosymbolic AI continue to be wildly underfunded, receiving, if I had to guess maybe 1%, probably less, of total investments
claim: Neurosymbolic AI approaches receive roughly 1% or less of total AI investment, vastly underfunded relative to LLMs

quote: some significant fraction of that science will be wrong, because of the inevitable tendency of LLMs to hallucinate
claim: A significant fraction of AI-assisted science will be wrong due to the inevitable tendency of LLMs to hallucinate

quote: The AI industry magic sauce of "scaling" hasn't made the enshittification situation better; it has made it worse
claim: Scaling LLMs has worsened rather than improved the problem of AI-generated low-quality content polluting the internet

quote: Cesspools of automatically-generated fake websites, rather than ChatGPT search, may ultimately come to be the single biggest threat that Google ever faces
claim: AI-generated fake websites may be a bigger threat to Google than ChatGPT search competition

quote: still part of the pack, not a giant leap forward (e.g. Grok 4 beats it on ARC-AGI-2 results)
claim: GPT-5 is part of the pack of current AI models, not a giant leap forward, with Grok 4 beating it on ARC-AGI-2

quote: GPT-5 is not the huge leap forward people long expected
claim: GPT-5 is not the huge leap forward that was widely expected

quote: GPT-5 is obviously not AGI
claim: GPT-5 is obviously not AGI

quote: Pricing is good, but profits may continue to be elusive; still no clear technical moat
claim: Despite good pricing, OpenAI profits may continue to be elusive and the company lacks a clear technical moat

quote: "AGI 2027" seems more and more remote by the day
claim: The prospect of achieving AGI by 2027 is becoming increasingly unlikely

quote: none of the problems I said would not be solved appear to have been solved
claim: GPT-5 has not solved any of the fundamental problems Marcus predicted would remain unsolved (hallucinations, reasoning errors, alignment)

quote: GPT-5 just not that different from anything that came before
claim: GPT-5 is not qualitatively different from previous models

quote: GPT-5 is barely better than last month's flavor of the month (Grok 4); on some metrics (ARC-AGI-2) it's actually worse
claim: GPT-5 is barely better than Grok 4 and actually worse on some metrics like ARC-AGI-2

quote: They no longer have anything like a clear technical lead
claim: OpenAI no longer has a clear technical lead over competitors
